parsedContent.instructions: Read all instructions carefully before you begin writing. Write your full name and index number on the answer booklet as they appear on your registration form. DO NOT write your names or index number on any additional answer sheets. Do not open this paper until you are instructed to do so. Use only a BLUE or BLACK pen for writing answers, and a PENCIL for drawings. Calculators and mathematical instruments may be used where necessary. Show all working steps for calculation questions to earn full marks. Diagrams should be clearly drawn with all labels. Crossing out and rewriting is allowed. Only the final answer will be marked.

answerKey.1: a) Magnetic pole b) Magnetic field c) Demagnetized
answerKey.2: a) iii – the medium through which the message is transmitted b) ii – Fiber optic cable c) ii – resistor, capacitor, transistor d) iv – ordinary diode
answerKey.3: a) iii – Fulcrum b) ii – load/effort c) ii – effort; load d) i – wheel and axle
answerKey.4: a) TRUE b) TRUE c) FALSE d) FALSE
answerKey.5: a) TRUE b) FALSE (potential energy converts to kinetic energy) c) FALSE (geothermal = heat from Earth's interior)
answerKey.6: a) iv – A = Negative, B = Positive (field lines go from positive to negative; arrows show B is positive, A is negative) b) iii – electric field intensity c) i – Q/(4πε₀ d) d) iii – paint spraying
answerKey.7: a) Speed → iv [LT⁻ ¹] b) Density → iii [ML⁻ ³] c) Acceleration → iv [LT⁻ ²] d) Energy → i [ML²T⁻ ²]
answerKey.8: a) Renewable energy sources can be naturally replenished in a short time and are available indefinitely (e.g., solar, wind). Non-renewable energy sources took millions of years to form and cannot be replaced once used (e.g., fossil fuels). b) i) Any one of: solar energy, wind power, hydroelectric power, geothermal energy, biomass ii) Any one of: coal, petroleum/oil, natural gas, uranium/nuclear fuel, minerals
answerKey.9: a) Statement C: At t=1s, a runner is moving at 4 m/s west b) It specifies both the speed (4 m/s), the direction (west), and the exact instant in time (t = 1 s). Instantaneous velocity requires speed, direction, and a specific point in time.
answerKey.10: a) Real transformers have losses due to: (i) resistance (copper) losses in the coil windings, and (ii) eddy current and hysteresis losses in the iron core / magnetic flux leakage. b) Thicker wires have lower electrical resistance (R = ρL/A; larger A = lower R). Lower resistance means less power lost as heat (P = I²R), making transmission more efficient. c) Step-down transformer (the distribution transformer reduces high transmission voltage to household level). d) Electricity at home is used for lighting, heating/cooling, powering appliances (TV, radio, computer, refrigerator, etc.).
answerKey.11: a) i) The pushing force is equal to or less than the friction force, so the net horizontal force is zero – the brick remains stationary. ii) The weight (downward force) is exactly balanced by the normal reaction force from the table surface (Newton's 3rd Law). b) i) Linear momentum P = mv increases, because the constant resultant force causes constant acceleration (Newton's 2nd Law: F=ma), increasing velocity over time. ii) The sliding brick generates heat (friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy) and/or produces sound.
answerKey.14: a) Impedance decreases. For a capacitor, Z = Xₙ = 1/(2πfC). As f increases, Z decreases (inversely proportional). b) rms current increases. Iᵣₘₛ = Vᵣₘₛ / Z = Vᵣₘₛ × 2πfC. As f increases, Iᵣₘₛ increases (directly proportional).
answerKey.15: Without the hydrosphere: There would be no liquid water on Earth's surface. Living cells could not carry out normal functions – all life would cease to exist. There would be no habitat for aquatic organisms (fish, crocodiles, etc.). No evaporation, so no clouds could form, meaning no rainfall and no precipitation. Desertification would cover the entire planet. The climate regulation provided by oceans would be lost, leading to extreme temperature variations.
answerKey.16: c) i) Power of converging lens: P₁ = 1/f = 1/0.25 m = +4 D ii) Power of diverging lens: P₂ = 1/f = 1/(-0.10 m) = -10 D iii) Total power: P = P₁ + P₂ = 4 + (-10) = -6 D iv) The combination is DIVERGING because the total power is negative (-6 D).
answerKey.17: b) Procedure: Connect the circuit as drawn. Close the switch. Set the rheostat to different positions to vary the current. For each setting, record the ammeter reading (I) and the voltmeter reading (V). Repeat for at least 5 different current values. c) Analysis: Plot a graph of V (y-axis) vs. I (x-axis). Calculate the slope of the best-fit line. The slope = V/I = R (resistance). If the graph is a straight line through the origin, Ohm's Law (V = IR) is verified. d) Safety precautions: (i) Do not leave the circuit connected for long periods to prevent overheating. (ii) Ensure all connections are tight to avoid sparks. (iii) Do not exceed the rated current of the components.
answerKey.18: b) i) 2500.0 ml ÷ 1000 = 2.5 L ii) 30 + 273 = 303 K iii) Charles's Law: V₁ /T₁ = V₂ /T₂ → V₂ = V₁ × T₂ /T₁ = 2.5 × 283/303 = 2.335 L ≈ 2.34 L c) Combined Gas Law: P₁ V₁ /T₁ = P₂ V₂ /T₂ → T₂ = T₁ × (P₂ V₂ )/(P₁ V₁ ) = 310 × (3.5×12)/(2.0×15) = 310 × 42/30 = 434 K d) Gay-Lussac's Law: P₁ /T₁ = P₂ /T₂ → T₁ = T₂ × P₁ /P₂ = 312.5 × 1.40/2.38 = 183.8 K
answerKey.19: a) i) Volume displaced = 10⁻ ³ m³ (same as balloon volume) ii) Weight = ρ × V × g = 1000 × 10⁻ ³ × 9.81 = 9.81 N (proved) iii) Buoyant force = 9.81 N. The balloon floats completely submerged because the buoyant force equals the weight of water displaced, which equals the weight of the balloon contents (water), so the net force is zero. b) i) Buoyant force = ρᵤᵃᵣᵉᵗᵉᴿ × V × g = 1000 × 10⁻ ³ × 9.81 = 9.81 N ii) Weight of wood = mg = 0.5 × 9.81 = 4.905 N iii) The wood will float PARTIALLY IMMERSED. Buoyant force (9.81 N) > Weight (4.905 N), so net upward force exists. Wood rises until buoyant force equals its weight. Since density of wood (0.5/0.001 = 500 kg/m³) < density of water (1000 kg/m³), it floats partially submerged.
answerKey.20: a) i) Solids: Conduction ii) Liquids: Convection b) Heat is a form of energy transferred due to a temperature difference, measured in Joules. Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a substance, measured in Kelvin or Celsius. c) i) Initial volume = length × width × height = 20 cm × 10 cm × 5 cm = 1000 cm³ = 1 × 10⁻ ³ m³ ii) Coefficient of volume expansion γ = 3α = 3 × 1.2 × 10⁻ ⁵ = 3.6 × 10⁻ ⁵ /°C iii) ΔV = γ × V₀ × ΔT = 3.6 × 10⁻ ⁵ × 1000 cm³ × (40−10) = 3.6 × 10⁻ ⁵ × 1000 × 30 = 1.08 cm³
answerKey.21: a) i) Mass: Balance / beam balance / electronic balance ii) Distance: Metre rule / measuring tape b) Formula: a = F/m (Newton's Second Law). Since mass is constant in this experiment, acceleration is directly proportional to force. Alternatively, from kinematics: a = (v² – u²) / (2s) or a = (v – u) / t using motion equations. c) Graph: Plot Force (N) on y-axis vs Acceleration (m/s²) on x-axis. Points should be approximately linear: (0.40, 0.20), (0.52, 0.25), (0.60, 0.30), (0.68, 0.35), (0.80, 0.40), (1.00, 0.50). Draw a straight best-fit line through the plotted points. d) i) The graph shows that force is directly proportional to acceleration (linear relationship through origin). As the acceleration increases, the force applied also increases proportionally. ii) Slope = ΔF/Δa = (0.50 – 0.20)/(1.00 – 0.40) = 0.30/0.60 = 0.5 kg. The slope represents the mass of the cart (m = F/a = slope). Mass of cart = 0.5 kg. e) The graph passed through all points because the experiment was performed under ideal/controlled conditions, the measurements were very precise, or systematic/random errors were minimized.

sections.name: A
sections.description: Multiple Choice, True/False, and Fill-in-the-Blank Questions

questions.text: Complete each sentence below using the most appropriate term from the box. Each term may be used once or not at all. Magnetized Magnetic field Demagnetized Magnetic pole

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: A ................................ is a region at each end of a magnet where the magnetic field is strongest.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The area around a magnet in which it can exert a magnetic force is called ................................

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: At around 80°C, a magnet will become ................................ permanently if exposed to this temperature for a prolonged period.

questions.text: Identify the answer choice that best completes each statement or answers each question.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The communication channel refers to:

options.text: the physical location where communication takes place
the medium through which the message is transmitted

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following cables transmits signals in the form of light?

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: The circuit shown in Figure 1 represents an amplifier circuit. The electronic components shown are:

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following electronic components is a semiconductor device?

questions.text: Complete the following statements by selecting the correct term from the given alternatives.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Which of the following is a fixed point about which a lever rotates or pivots?

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: The Mechanical Advantage of a simple machine is:

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: The Velocity Ratio of a simple machine is the ratio of the distance travelled by the ......... to the distance travelled by the ......... in the same machine.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: All vehicles move with the help of the simple machine called:

questions.text: State whether each of the following statements is TRUE or FALSE.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: The pressure exerted by a static liquid increases linearly with increasing depth.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Ice floats on water because ice has a lower density than water.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A force acting on a larger surface area of an object exerts a larger pressure.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: Relative density of a substance is the ratio of the density of water to the density of the substance.

questions.text: State whether each of the following statements is TRUE or FALSE.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: A car parked at the top of a hill possesses potential energy.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: A ball dropped from a height h towards the earth surface is an example of kinetic energy being converted into potential energy.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Geothermal energy is heat produced by burning natural gas.

questions.text: When light passes through a lens, it is refracted at both faces of the lens.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Copy the diagram (Figure 7) into your answer booklet and complete the path of the light ray through the section of the lens. Include the normal line at both faces.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Draw a ray diagram to show the formation of a virtual image in a converging lens.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A converging lens of focal length 25 cm and a diverging lens of focal length -10 cm are placed in contact.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the converging lens.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the diverging lens.

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Calculate the power of the combination of these lenses.

subQuestions.label: iv)
subQuestions.text: Is the system formed by this combination converging or diverging? Explain by ignoring the lens thickness.

questions.text: You are provided with the following electrical circuit components: connecting wires, 1 ammeter, 1 voltmeter, 1 resistor, 1 rheostat, 2 dry cells, and 1 switch/plug key.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Draw an electric circuit diagram showing the arrangement of the given laboratory materials.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: State the procedures to be followed and the measurements to be taken.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Analyse the data collected during measurements.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: State at least TWO safety precautions to be taken during the experiment.

questions.text: An ideal gas is a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no interactions, and which consequently obeys the gas laws exactly.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Draw a graph that illustrates Boyle's Law of ideal gas.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: A balloon has a volume of 2,500.0 ml of helium on a day when the temperature is 30.0 °C.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Convert 2,500.0 ml to litres (L).

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Convert 30.0 °C to Kelvin (K).

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: If the temperature at night falls to 283.0 K, what will be the new volume of the balloon if the pressure remains constant?

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: A pure helium gas is contained in a leak-proof cylinder with a movable piston.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: The initial volume, pressure, and temperature of the gas are 15 litres, 2.0 atm, and 310 K respectively.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: If the volume is decreased to 12 litres and the pressure is increased to 3.5 atm, find the final temperature of the gas.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: A sample of gas in a rigid container has a pressure of 1.40 atm.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Its temperature is increased to 312.5 K and the pressure is measured to be 2.38 atm.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: What was the initial temperature before heating?

questions.text: Archimedes' Principle states that a body immersed in a fluid experiences an upthrust (buoyant force) and this is fundamental to the equilibrium of a body floating in motionless water.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Consider a massless balloon filled with 10⁻ ³ m³ (1000 cm³) of water in a container of water.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What is the volume of water displaced by the balloon?

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Show that the weight of the water displaced in the container is 9.81 N.

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: What is the buoyant force acting on the balloon? Explain using the figure that shows the body floating completely submerged just below the surface of the liquid.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Assume the balloon is replaced by a 0.5 kg piece of wood that has exactly the same volume (1000 cm³ = 10⁻ ³ m³).

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What is the buoyant force acting on the piece of wood just after it was released?

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: What is the weight of the piece of wood in air?

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Just after releasing the piece of wood, will it float partially immersed, float completely submerged, or sink? Explain your answer.

questions.text: Thermal expansion is the tendency of matter to change in shape, volume, and area in response to a change in temperature.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Identify the general mode of heat transfer in solids and liquids.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Solids:

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Liquids:

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Differentiate between heat and temperature

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: The temperature of an iron block measuring 20 cm × 10 cm × 5 cm is raised from 10°C to 40°C.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Find the initial volume of the iron block.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Find the coefficient of volume expansion of the iron block.

subQuestions.label: iii)
subQuestions.text: Find the change in volume of this iron block.

questions.text: In an experiment to investigate the relationship between the force on an object and the acceleration it experiences, a learner used the experimental set-up shown in Figure 10.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Identify the measuring instrument used to measure the following physical quantities:

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Mass:

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Distance:

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Provide a formula used to determine the acceleration of the cart. Justify your answer.

subQuestions.label: c)
subQuestions.text: Plot a graph of the force F applied to the body (y-axis) against its acceleration a (x-axis). Draw the best-fit line on your graph paper.

subQuestions.label: d)
subQuestions.text: What does your graph tell you about the relationship between the acceleration of the body and the force applied to it? Support your answer.

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: What does your graph tell you about the relationship between the acceleration of the body and the force applied to it?

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Determine the slope of your graph. Interpret your result.

subQuestions.label: e)
subQuestions.text: On a trial run of this experiment, a learner found that the graph did go through all plotted points.
